text: Mordaunt Fenwick-Bisset (c. 1825 – 1884) was a British Member of Parliament for West Somerset. He was also Master of the Devon and Somerset Staghounds from 1855 to 1881, and was instrumental in increasing wild red deer numbers on Exmoor.

The Navy Midshipmen men's lacrosse team, representing the United States Naval Academy, was founded in 1908 and competes in the Patriot League. The team plays its home games at the Navy–Marine Corps Memorial Stadium in Annapolis, Maryland, and is currently coached by Joe Amplo. Historically, the Midshipmen have achieved significant success, winning 17 national championships prior to the NCAA era and finishing as NCAA tournament runners-up twice. The program has also made 27 NCAA tournament appearances and has a strong tradition of excellence, including multiple conference championships since joining the Patriot League in 2004.

Marco Pinotti (born February 25, 1976) is a retired Italian professional road cyclist known for his expertise in time trials. Nicknamed "L'Ingegnere" (The Engineer) and "Il Professore" (The Professor), he achieved significant success, including two individual stage wins at the Giro d'Italia and multiple national time trial championships.

"None but One" (1977) is a folk and folk rock album by American singer-songwriter Jean Ritchie. Produced by Al Steckler and Ron Frangipane, it blends traditional Appalachian music with electric instrumentation. The album includes social commentary tracks, such as an environmental hymn later adopted by anti-mountaintop removal activists. Despite distribution issues due to Sire Records' label transition, it became Ritchie's most acclaimed work, earning praise from *Rolling Stone* and *Melody Maker*. The album's success led Ritchie to establish her own label, Greenhays Recordings.

Skeletonization is the process by which a body decomposes to its skeletal remains, influenced by environmental factors such as temperature, humidity, and insect activity. In temperate climates, it typically takes weeks to years, while extreme conditions can delay or prevent it. Forensic anthropologists analyze skeletal remains to determine time of death, biological traits (age, sex), and trauma. Proper preservation and ethical considerations are crucial in handling skeletal remains for forensic, archaeological, or educational purposes.

Eddy L. Harris, born on January 26, 1956, in Indianapolis, is a writer and filmmaker known for works exploring African-American identity and travel. His books, including Mississippi Solo and Native Stranger, have garnered recognition and controversy. Harris now lives in France and continues to produce documentaries, aiming to foster unity through understanding.
George Petrakos is a Strength and Conditioning Coach known for his work in rugby, football, and hockey.

The University of Florida (UF) clinical toxicology distance education program, based in Gainesville, offers both a Master of Science (MS) program and a graduate certificate in clinical toxicology. The MS program, consisting of 32 credits, caters to health science professionals seeking to enhance their knowledge in toxicology and medical principles. The certificate program, available entirely online and comprising 15 credits, provides a background in toxicants commonly encountered in poison control centers and emergency departments, requiring a bachelor's degree in a relevant field. Both programs are managed by the UF College of Pharmacy and are accessible through the website clintox.cop.ufl.edu.
Lagoa do Parado Municipal Nature Park is a protected area in Guaratuba, Paraná, characterized by its tropical super-humid climate, rich biodiversity, and significant marine life breeding grounds.

Furcifer tuzetae, named in honor of Odette Tuzet, is a chameleon species found in Madagascar's dry forest in Befandriana Sud. Classified as Data Deficient by the IUCN, it is likely facing population decline due to habitat loss from slash-and-burn agriculture and logging. Described in 1972, it was later transferred to the genus Furcifer.
The Colored Hockey League (CHL) was founded in 1895 in Nova Scotia, Canada by Black leaders to attract young Black men to Sunday worship. The league had teams in Nova Scotia and Prince Edward Island.

The Church of Nossa Senhora do Rosário in Calheta, São Jorge, Azores, Portugal, is a Roman Catholic church dedicated to the Virgin Mary. Constructed in the 16th century, it was significantly damaged by the 1757 earthquake and later reconstructed. The church features a single-story nave with a massive pediment and a two-storey bell tower. Notable interior elements include gilded retables and Baroque decorations.

Washing Machine is the 1995 studio album by Sonic Youth, released on September 26, 1995, through DGC Records. Recorded from January to May 1995, the album marked a return to the band's experimental rock and noise rock roots, with a focus on lengthy, complex compositions. It follows Sonic Youth's 1994 album Experimental Jet Set, Trash and No Star and precedes SYR1: Anagrama (1997). The album's standout track, "The Diamond Sea," is notable for its 19:35 duration. Washing Machine received critical acclaim for its adventurous and challenging approach, with praise directed towards the band's innovative guitar interplay and the album's overall maturity. It debuted at No. 58 on the US Billboard 200 and No. 39 on the UK Albums Chart, selling 159,000 copies in the US by 2005.

Leigh Diffey, born on March 3, 1971, in Brisbane, Queensland, Australia, is an Australian-American sports commentator active since 1995. He began his career in motorsports broadcasting in Australia, working for networks like Network Ten, BBC, and Speed Channel. In 2012, he joined NBC Sports, becoming the play-by-play announcer for Formula One and IndyCar events. He has also covered NASCAR, Global Rallycross, and various Olympic sports, including luge, skeleton, and bobsled. Diffey obtained U.S. citizenship in 2011, reflecting his deep connection to the country that has supported his career.

Jody Hamilton (born Joseph Nicholas Zwaduk III; August 28, 1938 – August 3, 2021) was an American professional wrestler, promoter, and trainer, best known for his work as "The Assassin" and later "The Flame." Debuting in 1955, Hamilton achieved fame as part of the masked tag team "The Assassins" alongside Tom Renesto, becoming one of the most notorious and successful tag teams in wrestling history. The duo held multiple championships, including a record 12 NWA Georgia Tag Team Championships, and headlined events worldwide. Hamilton also found success as a singles competitor, holding titles such as the NWA Central States Heavyweight Championship.

After retiring from in-ring competition in the 1980s due to a back injury, Hamilton became a prominent trainer, running World Championship Wrestling's Power Plant, where he trained stars like Bill Goldberg and Kevin Nash. He later partnered with WWE to establish Deep South Wrestling as a developmental territory. Hamilton also worked with Rampage Pro Wrestling and was involved in a legal dispute with WWE, which was settled in 2010. 

Hamilton's son, Nick Patrick, followed in his footsteps as a wrestling referee. Hamilton passed away on August 3, 2021, at the age of 82, leaving behind a legacy as a wrestler, trainer, and influential figure in professional wrestling.

Washington Bogart Cooper (1802–1888) was a Tennessee-born painter who settled in Nashville. Known for his portraits of Tennessee governors and other notable figures, he produced over 35 portraits annually from 1837 to 1848. Cooper also worked for religious and fraternal organizations. He married Ann Litton in 1839 and had four children.

Germania was a German airline founded in April 1978 as Special Air Transport (SAT), commencing operations on September 5, 1978. Renamed Germania in 1986, it operated charter services and, later, scheduled flights. Germania briefly operated as a low-cost airline named "gexx" in the early 2000s. The airline expanded with bases in the UK and plans to modernize its fleet. However, after experiencing heavy losses, Germania ceased operations on February 5, 2019, and declared bankruptcy. At the time of its shutdown, Germania had a fleet of 36 aircraft and served 42 destinations.

Kalopanax is a genus of trees in the Araliaceae family, with the species Kalopanax septemlobus being the most notable. This tree can grow up to 30 meters tall and features spiny stems and large, palmately lobed leaves. It is cultivated for its ornamental value and is hardy in cold climates. In Korea, young shoots are consumed as a dish called sukhoe.

SLINEX is a biennial naval exercise between India and Sri Lanka, conducted off the coast of Trincomalee, Sri Lanka. Notable editions include SLINEX-13 (2013), SLINEX-15 (2015), SLINEX-18 (2018), and SLINEX-20 (2020), involving various ships and aircraft from both navies.

Carmen Contreras-Bozak (1919–2017) was the first Hispanic woman to serve in the Women's Army Corps (WAAC) during World War II. She transmitted encoded messages at General Eisenhower's headquarters in Algiers.

The 2020–21 UEFA Women's Champions League knockout phase took place from December 9, 2020, to May 16, 2021, featuring 32 teams. Matches were played over two legs, with aggregate scores determining advancement. Notable outcomes included Barcelona and Chelsea reaching the final, held at Gamla Ullevi in Gothenburg, where they competed for the title.

Wergea (Dutch: Warga) is a village in the municipality of Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands, with a population of 1,830 as of 2021. Originating as a terp village in the early Middle Ages, it developed into a center of trade and shipping during the 16th and 17th centuries. Notable landmarks include the 1863 polder mill De Hempenserpoldermolen and the Roman Catholic St. Martinus Church, built between 1860 and 1862. Wergea was also the site of the Netherlands' first cooperative dairy factory, established in 1886. Previously part of Boarnsterhim municipality, it became part of Leeuwarden in 2014.
Ludovike Simanowiz (1759–1827) was a German portrait painter, known for her miniatures and her close association with Friedrich Schiller. She studied in Paris and painted notable figures of her time.

The Neradje Mosque (built 1890) is a former mosque in Rethymno, Crete, Greece. It was built by Georgios Daskalakis during Ottoman rule. It previously was a Catholic monastery. Today it is a music school.

Ivy Winters, born Dustin Winters on October 5, 1986, is an American drag performer, singer, and actor, best known for competing on season 5 of RuPaul's Drag Race, where he was named Miss Congeniality. He has a background in circus performance and costume design.

The 2018 New Hampshire elections were held on November 6, 2018, alongside the primary elections held on June 5, 2018. Incumbent Republican Chris Sununu was reelected as governor. Both of New Hampshire's seats in the United States House of Representatives were up for election and retained by the Democratic Party. Democrats gained control of the State Senate, Executive Council, and House of Representatives.

Velocity Tower is a completed residential skyscraper located in Sheffield, South Yorkshire, standing at an estimated height of 92 meters. Construction began in 2006, and the tower opened in December 2008, featuring a modern glass façade and 30 planned floors.

*Fairy Tales Told for Children. First Collection.* (Danish: *Eventyr, fortalte for Børn. Første Samling.*) is a series of three booklets by Hans Christian Andersen, published between 1835 and 1837. The first booklet included *The Tinderbox*, *Little Claus and Big Claus*, *The Princess and the Pea*, and *Little Ida's Flowers*. The second introduced *Thumbelina*, *The Naughty Boy*, and *The Travelling Companion*, while the third contained *The Little Mermaid* and *The Emperor's New Clothes*. Initially met with criticism for their informal style, these tales later became foundational works of literary fairy tales.

Todd Compton is an American scholar and author known for his work in Mormon history, particularly his book *In Sacred Loneliness: The Plural Wives of Joseph Smith*. He holds a Ph.D. in classics from UCLA and has taught at various universities.

Fuxi (伏羲), a prominent figure in Chinese mythology, is regarded as one of the Three Sovereigns and a cultural hero. Often depicted with a human face and a snake's body, he is said to have been born alongside his sister Nüwa and played a key role in the creation of humanity. Fuxi is credited with introducing marriage, cooking, fishing, hunting, and the I Ching trigrams. He is also associated with the invention of calligraphy and the Guqin musical instrument.

The "San Lorenzo March" is a military march composed by Cayetano Alberto Silva in 1901, dedicated to Argentine general José de San Martín and the Battle of San Lorenzo. The march was first publicly performed on October 30, 1902, during the opening of a monument to San Martín in Rosario. Lyrics were later added by Carlos Javier Benielli in 1908, describing the battle and the heroism of Juan Bautista Cabral. Over time, the march gained international acclaim and is recognized as one of the finest military marches, performed by various military bands worldwide.

The 400th Missile Squadron was a unit of the United States Air Force that served in various roles from 1942 to 2005. Initially established as the 10th Reconnaissance Squadron during World War II, it was soon renamed the 400th Bombardment Squadron and operated B-24 Liberators in the Southwest Pacific Theater. The squadron conducted bombing missions in New Guinea, the Philippines, and other Pacific regions, earning three Distinguished Unit Citations and the Philippine Presidential Unit Citation for its actions. It was inactivated in 1946 after the war.

Reactivated in 1964 as an intercontinental ballistic missile squadron at Francis E. Warren Air Force Base, Wyoming, the 400th operated Minuteman I and III missiles before becoming the only squadron to deploy the LGM-118 Peacekeeper missile in 1986. The squadron received the General Samuel C. Phillips Award in 1999 for excellence. Following the retirement of the Peacekeeper system, the 400th Missile Squadron was inactivated on October 1, 2005.

"D.A.N.C.E." is a nu-disco single by Justice from their album †, released on April 23, 2007. Inspired by Michael Jackson, the song features interpolations from several of his tracks and was dedicated to him. It was a hit, reaching number 4 on Rolling Stone's list of the 100 Best Songs of 2007. The music video, directed by Jonas & François and animated by So Me, won the MTV Europe Music Award for "Video of the Year" and is known for its pop art style and morphing imagery.

Steven Williams (born January 7, 1949) is an American actor known for his extensive work in film and television since 1975. He gained prominence for his role as Captain Adam Fuller on the Fox series *21 Jump Street* (1987–1991) and as X on the science fiction series *The X-Files*, earning a Screen Actors Guild nomination in 1997. Williams has also appeared in *The Chi*, *Supernatural*, and *Locke & Key*, among other series. His film credits include *The Blues Brothers* (1980) and *Jason Goes to Hell: The Final Friday* (1993). Married to talent agent Ann Geddes, he has two daughters.

"Forever and for Always" is a song by Canadian singer Shania Twain, released on April 7, 2003, as the third single from her fourth studio album, *Up!* (2002). Written and produced by Twain and her then-husband Robert John "Mutt" Lange, the country-pop ballad celebrates enduring love. The song was inspired by relationships that last a lifetime, a theme reflected in its music video, which was filmed at Bethells Beach, New Zealand, and depicts a couple's journey from childhood to old age. The video won Female Video of the Year at the 2004 CMT Flameworthy Awards.

Critically acclaimed for its heartfelt theme, the song achieved significant commercial success. It peaked at number four on the Billboard Hot Country Songs chart and number one on the Adult Contemporary chart, where it remained for six weeks. Internationally, it reached the top 10 in six countries, including the UK. Twain revealed in 2019 that Prince had recorded an unreleased version of the song.

Charles Boarman (December 24, 1795 – September 13, 1879) was a Rear Admiral in the United States Navy. He served from 1811 to 1876, participating in the War of 1812 and the American Civil War.

Born in Bryantown, Maryland, Boarman came from a family with deep roots in colonial Maryland. He was educated at Georgetown College before joining the Navy as a midshipman. He served in the Mediterranean, West Indies, and Brazil squadrons, commanding several ships, including the USS Weasel, USS Java, and USS Brandywine.

Boarman's career included anti-piracy operations in the West Indies and a three-year voyage with the Brazil Squadron. From 1852 to 1855, he was Commandant of the Brooklyn Navy Yard, where he oversaw the fitting out of the Japan expedition under Commodore Matthew C. Perry.

Despite his Southern origins, Boarman remained loyal to the Union during the Civil War. He was appointed to the U.S. Naval Board in 1863 and promoted to commodore in 1867.

Boarman retired as a rear admiral and returned to Martinsburg, West Virginia, where he died in 1879. The Boarman House, his family home in Martinsburg, is a historical landmark. At the time of his death he was the longest serving officer in the U.S. Navy Register, with over 68 years of service.

The Irish Centre for High-End Computing (ICHEC), established in 2005, is Ireland's national high-performance computing center, headquartered in Dublin and Galway. Operated by NUI Galway, ICHEC manages supercomputers, including Kay and its predecessor Fionn, and conducts research in computational science, hardware acceleration, and big data analytics.

Eskişehirspor is a Turkish professional football club based in Eskişehir. Founded in 1965 through a merger of local teams, they were quickly promoted to the First League. The club was a strong Anatolian team in the late 1960s and early 1970s, finishing as Süper Lig runners-up three times and winning the Turkish Cup in 1970. Currently, they play in the TFF Third League. Their home ground is the New Eskişehir Stadium, which has a capacity of 32,500.

The Nieuport-Macchi Parasol was an Italian parasol wing monoplane designed and manufactured by Nieuport-Macchi, entering service in 1913. It was derived from the Nieuport IV, and was primarily constructed of wood and canvas. The Royal Italian Army employed the Parasol for artillery spotting during World War I, before retiring it from service in November 1915 due to a high accident rate.

"Casa Tomada" is a short story by Argentine author Julio Cortázar that explores themes of isolation and the unknown. The narrative follows a male narrator and his sister, Irene, both in their early forties, who inherit their parents' house and live a quiet, routine life marked by personal losses and unfulfilled relationships. The siblings engage in mundane activities, with Irene knitting and the narrator collecting French literature. Their tranquil existence is disrupted when the narrator hears mysterious noises from another part of the house, prompting them to retreat to a smaller section, locked away from the intruders. As the sounds grow more ominous, they become increasingly fearful and resigned to their fate. Ultimately, the siblings are forced to abandon their home, leaving behind their belongings, as the source of the disturbances remains a mystery. The story has been interpreted as a commentary on political and social issues in Argentina, particularly in relation to anti-Peronism.
Gaylord Dewayne Shaw (July 22, 1942 – September 6, 2015) was an American journalist known for winning a Pulitzer Prize in 1978 and breaking the news of President Nixon's resignation.

"Songs of the Pixies" is a poem by Samuel Taylor Coleridge written during his 1793 vacation in Devon. It draws on local folklore and references Coleridge's interactions with young ladies at The Pixies' Parlour. The poem, though not widely praised, showcases Coleridge's inventive powers and his early engagement with themes of fairy lore, influencing his later poetic style.

The Fruitmarket Gallery is a contemporary art gallery in Edinburgh, Scotland, established in 1974 in a former fruit and vegetable market building. Renovated in 1994 and expanded in 2021, it hosts international and Scottish art exhibitions, alongside events, a café, and a bookshop. Admission is free.

Hugh Cecil Lowther, 5th Earl of Lonsdale (1857–1944), served as Lord Lieutenant of Cumberland from 1917 to 1944. He inherited vast wealth from his father's coalmines and owned extensive land. Known for his extravagant lifestyle, he was involved in various pursuits including boxing, hunting, and horse racing.
The Toro Rosso STR10 is a Formula One racing car designed by Toro Rosso for the 2015 season, featuring a Renault V6 turbo engine and notable drivers Max Verstappen and Carlos Sainz, Jr.

The Mourne Mountains are a granite mountain range located in County Down, Northern Ireland, with the highest peak being Slieve Donard at 850 meters (2,790 feet). The name "Mourne" is derived from the Gaelic clan Múghdhorna, and the mountains are known for their distinctive peaks, many of which begin with "Slieve." The area is recognized for its natural beauty, including the Mourne Wall, a 31.4 km dry stone wall that crosses fifteen summits, and several reservoirs that supply water to Belfast. The Mournes are a popular destination for outdoor activities such as hiking, rock climbing, and have inspired various cultural references, including songs and literature.

The *Christmas in Spiceworld Tour* was a 1999 concert tour by the Spice Girls, held exclusively in the United Kingdom from December 4 to December 15. Featuring eight shows, the tour marked the group's return after a hiatus, showcasing an elaborate stage design with Christmas-themed elements. The performances were divided into three sections, blending hits, new songs, and festive medleys. A full concert at Earl's Court was broadcast live on Sky One, including behind-the-scenes footage. The tour was marred by the death of a crew member during set dismantling.

Peter Blundell Jones, born near Exeter, England, is known for his contributions to architecture and journalism. He attended Blundell’s School in Tiverton and later studied at the Architectural Association (AA). After working as an assistant lecturer at Cambridge University (1979-1983) and teaching at the South London Polytechnic, he was appointed professor at the University of Sheffield School of Architecture in 1994. Blundell Jones is recognized for his architectural designs, including The Round House at Stoke Canon and Padley Mill, and for his writings on architects like Scharoun. He was named architectural journalist of the year in 1992.

W. & J. Sloane was a prominent American retail company specializing in rugs, furniture, and home furnishings, founded in 1843 by William Sloane in New York City. Renowned for introducing oriental carpets to the U.S. and setting national decorating trends, it served elite clients, including the White House. After decades of success, the company declined following its 1955 acquisition, filing for bankruptcy in 1985.

Europe is a station on Line 3 of the Paris Métro, located in the 8th arrondissement of Paris. Opened on 19 October 1904, it is named after Place de l'Europe, a square from which streets named for European capitals radiate. The station underwent renovations in 2000 and was culturally developed for the metro's centenary. In 2018, it was renamed Europe - Simone-Veil to honor Simone Veil, a prominent French politician and Holocaust survivor. The station features a Guimard entrance, designated a historical monument, and is served by several RATP bus and Noctilien night bus lines.

Caesium bisulfate (CsHSO4) is a superionic conductor that exhibits significant proton conductivity above 141 °C, transitioning through three crystalline phases. Its unique properties make it suitable for potential applications in electrochemical devices.
Giorgio Zur (1930–2019) was a German archbishop and Vatican diplomat who served as Apostolic Nuncio to several countries, including Russia and Austria.

Lefty Leifield (September 5, 1883 – October 10, 1970) was an American professional baseball pitcher who played 15 seasons in Major League Baseball (MLB), primarily for the Pittsburgh Pirates. A 1909 World Series champion, he won 124 career games and was the last surviving member of the Pirates' championship team.

Rami Mehmed Pasha (1645–1706) was an Ottoman statesman who served as Grand Vizier from January to August 1703 and later as the governor of Egypt. Notable for his diplomatic role in the Treaty of Karlowitz, he faced challenges during his brief tenure as Grand Vizier, ultimately being deposed amid civil unrest.

Stonestown Galleria station is a light rail station on the Muni Metro M Ocean View line in San Francisco, California. Located at 19th Avenue and Winston Drive near Stonestown Galleria, it opened in 1925 and was rebuilt in 1993 with an accessible island platform. The station serves as a key transit hub with connections to multiple Muni bus routes and SamTrans.

Hostile vehicle mitigation (HVM) refers to security measures designed to prevent or reduce the impact of vehicle-based attacks, such as vehicle ramming or vehicle-borne improvised explosive devices (VBIEDs). Common HVM solutions include crash-tested barriers, bollards, reinforced street furniture, and architectural features integrated into urban landscapes. These systems are tested to withstand specific vehicle impacts and are often implemented with protective standoff distances to mitigate blast effects. HVM is used to safeguard buildings, crowded spaces, and high-value targets, such as museums and jewelry stores, while maintaining pedestrian accessibility and urban functionality.

A lord proprietor was an individual granted extensive powers over a proprietary colony, typically by a monarch, allowing them to govern, create laws, and appoint officials. This system became prominent in the British colonial era, particularly following Maryland's Royal Grant in 1632.

WNED-FM, branded as WNED Classical, is a superpower Class B FM radio station broadcasting at 94.5 MHz with HD Radio capabilities in Buffalo, New York, and extending into Toronto and parts of Western New York. Owned by Buffalo Toronto Public Media (formerly Western New York Educational Television Association), WNED-FM first aired on June 6, 1960, as WEBR-FM, transitioning through various call signs and formats before settling on classical music in 1976. The station offers a wide range of local programming and collaborates with local orchestras and ensembles. As a superpower station, WNED-FM broadcasts at 94,000 watts, making its signal extend into Canada.

DWOW, branded as K-Lite 103.5, is a radio station based in Mandaluyong, Philippines, broadcasting on 103.5 MHz with a frequency of 25,000 watts. Owned by Advanced Media Broadcasting System, it has undergone multiple format changes since its first air date on December 18, 1979. Notable formats include adult contemporary, OPM, and dance music. Currently, it operates as a top 40 station with the slogan "The Hits of the 90s and 2K, Today."

Walter G. Alexander (1880–1953) was an American physician and politician. He was the first African American to serve in the New Jersey Legislature, elected to the General Assembly in 1920. He was also active in the NAACP and the National Medical Association.
The **Belgium–Korea Treaty of 1901** was a commercial treaty negotiated between representatives of the Kingdom of Belgium and Korea.

Time's Up is an organization that raises money to support victims of sexual harassment. It was founded on January 1, 2018, by female Hollywood celebrities in response to the Harvey Weinstein sexual abuse cases and the Me Too movement. The organization publicly launched in January 2018 with an open letter in *The New York Times* and *La Opinión*, announcing the Time's Up Legal Defense Fund, administered by the National Women's Law Center.

The organization's mission is to advocate and campaign to address discrimination and harassment in workplaces. Time's Up has faced criticism regarding its focus on Hollywood, the diversity of its representatives, and potential conflicts of interest. In 2021, Time's Up faced turmoil after allegations surfaced that its chairwoman, Roberta Kaplan, was involved in discrediting an accuser of then-New York Governor Andrew Cuomo, leading to Kaplan's resignation, followed by the resignation of CEO Tina Tchen. Subsequently, Time's Up dissolved its advisory board, announced the resignation of its governing board members, and planned to replace its staff.

Hot Springs, Montana, located at 47°36′32′′N 114°40′19′′W, is a town in Sanders County, Montana, known for its mineral hot springs and named "Limp in, Leap out." With a population of 557 (2020 census), it covers 0.38 square miles. The town features a vibrant cultural scene with the Hot Springs Artists Society and LaRue Hot Springs Museum, and offers educational services through the Hot Springs School District. Transportation includes the Hot Springs Airport and local bus services provided by Sanders County Transportation.

Paul Speratus (1484–1551) was a Lutheran minister and hymn writer who played a crucial role in the early development of Lutheran hymnody. Ordained as a Roman Catholic priest, he converted to Lutheranism and became a key figure in the Reformation, helping to compile the first Lutheran hymnal, the Achtliederbuch, and writing influential hymns such as "Es ist das Heil uns kommen her." He served as court chaplain and Protestant bishop of Pomesania, defending Lutheran teachings until his death in 1551.

"System of a Down" is the debut studio album by the American metal band System of a Down, released on June 30, 1998. The album features a blend of nu metal and alternative metal, with lyrics addressing themes such as anti-war sentiments, genocide, and religious extremism. It includes notable singles like "Sugar," "Spiders," and "War," and received critical acclaim for its innovative sound and lyrical depth.

"Iced Out Audemars" is a drill song by American rapper Pop Smoke featuring Dafi Woo, released on the deluxe edition of his posthumous debut album *Shoot for the Stars, Aim for the Moon* (2020). Produced by Rico Beats, the track showcases Pop Smoke and Dafi Woo rapping about luxury over a glimmering beat. A remix featuring Lil Wayne was later released, where Wayne pays tribute to Pop Smoke, who was fatally shot earlier that year. Critics praised Lil Wayne's verse for its dynamic flow and homage to the late rapper. The song received positive reviews, with praise for its energetic production and Wayne's contribution.

Uyirmei is a Tamil soap opera medical series produced by Global One Studios and aired on Zee Tamil from August 18, 2014, to January 30, 2015. Created by Bhushan Kalyan and directed by Sundar K. Vijayan and Bhushan, the show stars Amala, Vineeth, Srithika, and Harshita. Set against the backdrop of Dayanand Medical Foundation, the plot revolves around Kavitha (Amala) managing the hospital after Dr. Dayananad's illness and subsequent death, while facing challenges from his family. The series ran for 122 episodes, each approximately 20-22 minutes long.

The Vilnius Girls' Gymnasium, established on January 1, 1860, was one of the first secondary schools for girls in the Russian Empire, initiated by the Ministry of National Education and financed by the Office of the Institutions of Empress Maria. Initially, the school faced low enrollment and restrictions, including a ban on Jewish students, which was lifted in 1861. The curriculum focused on Russian language and literature, with limited subjects compared to boys' schools. Over the years, the gymnasium adapted to increasing student numbers and community demands, eventually allowing classes in Judaism and expanding its curriculum to include Polish and Lithuanian languages after the Russian Revolution of 1905.

Sylvia Fine (August 29, 1913 – October 28, 1991) was an American composer, lyricist, and producer. She is best known as the wife of Danny Kaye, whose career she managed and for whom she wrote many songs. Fine also worked in television, earning a Peabody Award for her series *Musical Comedy Tonight*.
Jean Garrabé was a French psychiatrist known for his contributions to psychiatry and psychoanalysis.
Franziska Junge (born 1981) is a German actor and singer, known for her work in theatre, film, and television.

Heinz Rein (1906–1991) was a German novelist and journalist who worked as a bank clerk and sports journalist before facing a writing ban under Nazi rule. After World War II, he contributed to the New Literature movement in East Germany but faced censorship and suspensions. Moving to West Germany, he became known for his works on the post-war period in Berlin, including the bestseller *Berlin Finale*.
Oliver Bäte (born [year]) is a German business executive who has served as CEO of Allianz since 2015, after holding leadership roles at McKinsey & Company and Allianz.
Deerfoot, born Lewis Bennett in 1828, was a Seneca族运动员，以在英国的长跑比赛中的出色表现闻名。他以其快速的比赛速度、独特的战术和引人注目的装扮吸引了大量粉丝，并赢得了多个比赛。尽管面临严格的赛程安排，他仍保持了世界顶级长跑选手的地位。deerfoot去世后，他的名声依旧广泛流传。

Cai Jinbiao (born 1954) is a Chinese former footballer who played as a defender. Known for his strong defensive skills and nicknamed "Steel Head," he played for Guangdong FC, helping them win their first league title in 1979, and represented the China national team from 1976 to 1982.

Operation Northern Falcon is an annual fuel transportation mission conducted by the Ukrainian Air Force to Greenland since 2009, under a memorandum of understanding with the Danish defence ministry. In 2019, 23 Ukrainian servicemen transported 700,000 liters of fuel from Thule Air Base to Station Nord in challenging Arctic conditions.

GLtron is a snake game developed by Andreas Umbach and maintained by GLtron.org since 1999. Available on multiple platforms including Linux, Windows, and mobile devices, it features various gameplay styles and arena sizes. Known for its graphics and sound, GLtron has received positive reviews and was downloaded over 1.5 million times from SourceForge.

The Space Force Association is a nonprofit organization founded in 2019 to support the U.S. Space Force through policy research, STEM scholarships, and veteran transition assistance. It is headquartered in Colorado Springs, Colorado.

The term "supine" refers to a grammatical form found in various languages, including Latin, Sanskrit, and several European languages. In Latin, there are two types of supines: the first supine, ending in -tum, indicates purpose or fate, while the second supine, ending in -ū, expresses purpose or extent. The concept of supine also appears in English as the to-infinitive, in Swedish for forming compound verb tenses, and in other languages like Romanian and Lithuanian, where it serves similar functions.

Klinga Church (Norwegian: Klinga kirke) is a parish church in Namsos, Trøndelag, Norway. Likely built in the 14th century, the original stave church was replaced in 1705, and then again in 1866 when the current church was consecrated. The present wooden long church was designed by Ernst Kulaas and Jacob Wilhelm Nordan. The church tower was rebuilt in 1948 after being dismantled during World War II. The church is listed as a Norwegian Cultural Heritage Site.

Marilyn Suiter is an American geoscientist recognized for her contributions to geosciences education and diversity. She earned her degrees from Franklin & Marshall College and Wesleyan University, and has held significant roles at the American Geosciences Institute and the National Science Foundation, promoting minority participation in the field.

The ships *Anne* and *Little James* were two vessels that played a significant role in the early history of Plymouth Colony. In 1623, *Anne*, a 140-ton supply ship, and *Little James*, a 44-ton pinnace, transported a large group of new settlers to the colony. *Anne*, captained by William Peirce, carried the majority of the passengers, while *Little James*, under the command of Emmanuel Altham and master mariner John Bridges, also carried cargo and about fifteen passengers.

*Little James* was intended to support the colony's fishing and trading activities, but it was plagued by misfortune, including a mutinous crew, a shipwreck, and ultimately, capture by Barbary pirates in 1625. The loss of *Little James* was a blow to the Pilgrims, hindering their trade with England. While *Anne* returned to England soon after its arrival, *Little James* was intended to remain in New England.

Shikantaza, a term derived from the Sino-Japanese reading of Zhǐguǎn dǎzuò, translates to "just sitting" and refers to a form of meditation primarily associated with Zen Buddhism. It emphasizes wholehearted engagement in zazen, or seated meditation, and is characterized by silent illumination, a practice articulated by the Caodong school of Chan. Dōgen, a key figure in Zen, used shikantaza to denote a state of open awareness and alertness during meditation, reflecting the reality of life without distraction or drowsiness.

Dominic Michaelis (1938–2015), a French architect and inventor, was a pioneering solar energy advocate and solar balloon innovator. He designed the first solar-powered balloon to cross the English Channel in 1981.
Ludovike Simanowiz was a German portrait painter known for her artistic talent and connections to Friedrich Schiller, with whom she grew up in Ludwigsburg.

The Arnot Art Museum in Elmira, New York, was founded by banker Matthias Hallenback Arnot and houses a significant collection of European and American art. Built in 1833 and expanded in the 1890s and 1982, the museum features works by notable artists such as William-Adolphe Bouguereau, James Carroll Beckwith, and Jean-François Millet. Located at 235 Lake Street, it is part of the Elmira Civic Historic District and designated as a contributing property.

Robert Žák (born May 6, 1966) is a former Czech football midfielder and manager. He played for Slavia Prague, Hradec Králové, and České Budějovice, amassing 111 appearances and 18 goals in the Czechoslovak First League before continuing his career in the newly formed Gambrinus liga. As a manager, Žák had stints with several clubs, including České Budějovice, Most, Bohemians Prague, and Litvínov, but faced challenges leading to multiple dismissals.

"Imagine" is a pop song by Tone Damli featuring Eric Saade, released on April 27, 2012, as a single from the album *Looking Back*. A music video, released on YouTube, depicts a love story between Damli and Saade in Ibiza. Damli and Saade performed the song on Norwegian television and during the VG Lista 2012 tour.

Anna Marie Tendler (born June 9, 1985) is an American makeup artist, writer, and designer known for her work in the beauty industry and her creative ventures. She gained prominence through her Tumblr blog and has collaborated with various media outlets, including MTV. Tendler is also the owner of Silk Parlor, a business specializing in handcrafted lampshades. She was previously married to comedian John Mulaney from 2014 to 2022.

Maria-Luise Rainer (born 23 April 1959) is an Italian former luger who competed from 1976 to 1988, winning multiple international medals, including a bronze at the 1979 FIL World Luge Championships and a silver at the 1980 European Championships. She also won the overall Luge World Cup title in women's singles during the 1985–86 season. Rainer later served as race director at the 2006 Winter Olympics and has held senior technical roles with the International Luge Federation.

The Aston Centre for Europe (ACE), established in 2009 at Aston University, is a research center co-directed by Professors Simon Green, John Gaffney, and Dr. Nathaniel Copsey. It focuses on European policy issues and collaborates with various European institutions for educational programs and training.

The Swallow Music Awards (Armenian: Ծիծեռնակ երաժշտական մրցանակաբաշխություն) is an annual Armenian music awards ceremony established in 2017 and presented by the Ministry of Culture of Armenia. Held at the Armenian National Academic Theatre of Opera and Ballet in Yerevan, the awards recognize excellence and outstanding achievement in various music genres, including classical, folk, and pop. Unlike other Armenian music awards, the Swallow Music Awards require artists to submit applications, with nominees determined based on these submissions. The awards have been hosted by prominent Armenian figures and have honored notable artists and contributors to Armenian music. However, the application process has sparked criticism from some artists.
Air Vice-Marshal Paul Luker CB, OBE, AFC, is a retired Royal Air Force officer. He commanded Joint Helicopter Command before serving in Afghanistan and retiring in 2006.

Harvey Livett (born 4 January 1997) is an English professional rugby league footballer who plays as a second-row or centre. The son of former player Peter Livett, he debuted for Warrington Wolves in 2017, later playing in the 2018 Challenge Cup Final. After loan spells and stints with Salford Red Devils and Huddersfield Giants, he earned selection for the England Knights in 2018, touring Papua New Guinea.

Bristol, Tennessee, is a city in Sullivan County, located in the northeastern corner of the state. Incorporated in 1856 and named after Bristol, England, the city is part of the Tri-Cities region and shares its downtown area with Bristol, Virginia, along State Street. Known as the "Birthplace of Country Music," Bristol was recognized by the U.S. Congress in 1998 for its pivotal role in the early development of country music, particularly through the 1927 Bristol Sessions, which featured artists like the Carter Family and Jimmie Rodgers. The Birthplace of Country Music Museum, opened in 2014, commemorates this legacy.

Bristol is home to the Bristol Motor Speedway, a major NASCAR venue, and hosts the annual Bristol Rhythm & Roots Reunion music festival. The city has a humid subtropical climate with cooler temperatures due to its elevation. As of the 2020 census, Bristol had a population of 27,147. The city operates its own school system, including Tennessee High School, and is served by the Bristol Police Department. Bristol's economy and culture are closely tied to its musical heritage, motorsports, and its unique position straddling the Tennessee-Virginia state line.

Bertrand Ndongo is a Cameroonian-born political activist and YouTuber based in Spain, known for his association with the far-right Vox party. After moving to Spain in 2010, he worked as a cleaner before becoming a political consultant. Ndongo has drawn attention for his controversial statements on immigration, gender issues, and Spanish politics.
Bharatpur is an assembly constituency in Murshidabad district in the Indian state of West Bengal.

**Uyirmei** is a Tamil soap opera medical series that aired on Zee Tamil from August 18, 2014, to January 30, 2015. Directed initially by Bhushan Kalyan and later by Sundar K. Vijayan, the show follows Kavitha, who is entrusted with managing a medical foundation after its founder, Dr. Dayananad, falls ill. The plot centers on her struggles against Dayananad's children, who seek control of the hospital. The series consists of 122 episodes, with a running time of approximately 20-22 minutes each.

OpenDataSoft, founded in 2011 by Jean-Marc Lazard, Franck Carassus, and David Thoumas, is a SaaS solution headquartered in Boston, MA, and Paris, France. The company promotes data sharing and visualization, offering services to both public and private entities worldwide. Notable milestones include seed funding in 2015, Series A funding in 2016, and the launch of "Data on Board," an annual data sharing summit. With over 280 customers globally, OpenDataSoft supports various sectors including energy, transportation, and government.

China's Special Envoy on the Middle East Issue, established in 2002, plays a crucial role in facilitating peace efforts in the Israeli-Palestinian conflict. The position reflects China's historical support for Palestinian causes and aims to enhance regional stability, with five envoys having served since its inception, each contributing to evolving diplomatic strategies.

The Integrated Traffic and Landscape Concept (IVL) for the Grand Duchy of Luxembourg is a national strategy developed by multiple government ministries and stakeholders to guide sustainable spatial and urban development. It aims to prevent urban sprawl by focusing growth around key municipalities near major transport routes, promoting public transportation use. The IVL outlines two future scenarios and five spatial development models to address Luxembourg's evolving demographic and economic needs.
HMS Rover was a British sloop-of-war launched in 1796 and wrecked on June 23, 1798, while convoying a transport to Quebec. Her crew was saved despite the ship's loss.
Hanna Atik (born February 7, 1959) is a Lebanese politician and former military commander of the Lebanese Forces during the Civil War.
Les Gandar (1919–1994) was a New Zealand politician, farmer, and diplomat who served as Minister of Education (1975–1978) and High Commissioner to the UK (1979–1982).
Gretta Kehoe-Quigley is a former Irish camogie player from Wexford who played as a center back. She won a Leinster senior championship in 1974 and an All-Ireland medal in 1975 alongside her sisters.

Robert Simpson Neighbors (November 3, 1815 – September 14, 1859) was an American soldier and Indian agent known for his efforts to protect Native American tribes in Texas, particularly the Comanche. After immigrating to Texas in 1836, he served in the Army of the Republic of Texas and later became an influential Indian agent, implementing the innovative "field system" to engage directly with tribes. Neighbors was assassinated in 1859, likely due to his advocacy for Native American rights.
John Birkbeck (dates unknown) was a British Quaker banker, caver, and mountaineer who explored Gaping Gill and co-founded the Alpine Club.

Glory Lefoshie Sedibe (May 16, 1953 – March 20, 1994), also known by aliases such as Comrade September, was a South African anti-apartheid activist, intelligence operative, and later an askari (enemy agent). Born in Pilgrim's Rest, Mpumalanga, Sedibe joined the African National Congress (ANC) and its military wing, Umkhonto we Sizwe, in 1977, rising to become head of intelligence for the Transvaal region after receiving training in East Germany and the Soviet Union. 

In 1986, Sedibe was abducted by apartheid police in Swaziland with the assistance of local authorities. Following months of torture, he became an askari, collaborating with the apartheid regime to capture and kill his former ANC comrades. He later served as a key state witness in the 1988 treason trial of anti-apartheid activist Ebrahim Ebrahim and others. Sedibe died under mysterious circumstances in 1994, with conflicting accounts attributing his death to poisoning or heart failure. His legacy remains controversial due to his defection and actions as an askari.

The 1907 Los Angeles Angels were led by pitcher Dolly Gray, who had multiple 20-win seasons for the team. First baseman Pop Dillon led the team with a .304 batting average, while right fielder Gavvy Cravath tied for the league lead with 45 doubles. Left fielder Walter Carlisle led the PCL with 14 home runs.

Springfield Central railway station is an elevated station on the Springfield line in Queensland, Australia, serving the suburb of Springfield Central. Opened on December 2, 2013, it was part of the Darra to Springfield Transport Corridor project. The station has an island platform, bus bays, and parking facilities.

The Yale Glee Club, founded in 1861, is the oldest collegiate choral ensemble at Yale University and one of the most renowned in the United States. Based in New Haven, Connecticut, the group consists of 70 to 90 singers, primarily undergraduates, with membership determined by annual auditions. Originally an all-male group, it became a mixed chorus in 1970 following Yale's admission of female students. The Glee Club is directed by a faculty member from the Yale School of Music, currently Jeffrey Douma, and is supported by student officers and an alumni association.

Known for its diverse repertoire, the Glee Club performs classical, folk, and contemporary works. It has toured domestically since 1866 and internationally since 1928, serving as "ambassadors of song" and engaging in outreach programs.

Bent Sæther, born on February 18, 1969, in Oslo, Norway, is a multi-instrumentalist, vocalist, and producer in the rock genre. He co-founded Motorpsycho in 1989 and has produced albums for notable Norwegian bands.

Arkady Adamovich Brish (May 14, 1917 – March 19, 2016) was a Soviet and Russian physicist and engineer. A graduate of Belarusian State University, he contributed to resistance efforts during World War II and later worked in Moscow. Brish received numerous prestigious awards, including the Hero of Socialist Labour and the Lenin Prize. A memorial plaque in his honor was installed at the NL Dukhov All-Russian Research Institute of Automation in 2017.

"Flash" is a song by Japanese girl group Perfume, released digitally on March 16, 2016, as a single from their album *Cosmic Explorer*. The song incorporates J-pop and EDM genres and was produced by Yasutaka Nakata. The choreography blends martial arts and Tai chi. Its music video, directed by Yusuke Tanaka, has garnered over 60 million views on YouTube.

Pneumodesmus is an extinct genus of myriapod, specifically the species Pneumodesmus newmani, which lived during the late Silurian to early Devonian periods, approximately 428 to 414 million years ago. Discovered in Scotland, this fossil is significant as it represents the earliest documented arthropod with a tracheal system, indicating an air-breathing capability. Resembling a millipede, P. newmani is notable for its preserved body segments and features, marking it as one of the first known oxygen-breathing terrestrial animals.

Jimmy McCulloch (4 June 1953 – 27 September 1979) was a Scottish musician and songwriter, best known as the lead guitarist for Paul McCartney's band Wings from 1974 to 1977. A prodigious talent, McCulloch began playing guitar at age 11 and gained early recognition with the psychedelic rock band One in a Million and later with Thunderclap Newman, whose 1969 hit "Something in the Air" topped the UK charts. He also played with Stone the Crows, John Mayall's Bluesbreakers, and the reformed Small Faces, among others.

During his time with Wings, McCulloch contributed notable tracks such as "Medicine Jar" and "Wino Junko." He later formed the band White Line and collaborated with various artists. Known for his melodic, blues-infused guitar style, McCulloch tragically died of heart failure due to morphine and alcohol poisoning at age 26. His legacy includes contributions to rock and blues music across multiple bands and projects.

The Spindle is a cryobot under development by Stone Aerospace, designed to melt through ice sheets to reach subglacial lakes, particularly Lake Vostok in Antarctica. It integrates advanced laser technology and autonomous navigation systems to collect samples and resurface. Unlike its predecessor, VALKYRIE, which used hot water jets, Spindle beams its laser directly into the ice. The vehicle features a radar system and intelligent algorithms for autonomous scientific sampling and navigation. Spindle aims to pave the way for future missions to icy moons like Europa, Enceladus, or Ganymede, where it could explore subsurface oceans, assess habitability, and search for biosignatures. The project includes developing a compact fission power plant for sustained operations in extreme environments.

Wawne is a village and civil parish in the East Riding of Yorkshire, England, with a population of 975 as of the 2011 census. First mentioned in the Domesday Book in 1086, Wawne's name is derived from an Old English word meaning "shaking ground." Historically, it was a large parish that included Sutton-on-Hull. The village features the Grade I listed parish church of St. Peter and has a rich history, including a ferry service across the River Hull that operated until 1946. Wawne has strong historical ties to the nearby village of Sutton on Hull.

The Maas–Hoffman model describes the relationship between crop yield and soil salinity using two linear equations. It is defined by the maximum yield (C), the slope (A) and intercept (B) of a descending line, and the salinity level (X). The model is used to assess crop tolerance to seawater. An inverted version of the model can be used for growth factors that negatively affect crop production at low values.

Merewether is a suburb of Newcastle, New South Wales, established in 1890, with a population of 10,964 as of the 2016 census. Known for its beaches, including Merewether Beach and the largest ocean baths in the Southern Hemisphere, it has a history rooted in coal mining and various industries.

The 2023 BAL qualification, held from 10 October to 27 November 2022, featured 21 teams across four groups in the Road to BAL tournament. Key withdrawals and rescheduling affected the groups, with final games played in Niamey, Yaoundé, Kampala, and Antananarivo. The top teams advanced to the Elite 16, and the West Division finals were held in Abidjan, Ivory Coast, while the East Division finals took place in Ellis Park Arena, Johannesburg, South Africa.

The Waldorf-Astoria Hotel & Residence Tower is a proposed residential and commercial building in Chicago, Illinois, scheduled for construction starting in 2026 and estimated to be completed by 2031. Designed by DeStefano & Partners and developed by The Prime Group, the building will stand 1,265 feet tall with 107 floors, featuring an antenna spire reaching 1,200 feet. The proposed hotel portion, managed by The Waldorf-Astoria Collection, would include 325 rooms and 300 luxury condominiums. The design aims to adhere to Chicago's architectural trends favoring taller, thinner structures. The first 31 floors will house a St. Regis Hotel, while the remaining floors will offer residential apartments, including 300 luxury units and six duplex penthouses.

Vellattanjur is a village in the Thrissur district of Kerala, India, governed by a Grama Panchayat. As of the 2001 Census, it had a population of 6,637, with 3,194 males and 3,443 females. The official languages are Malayalam and English, and the village falls under the Alathur Lok Sabha constituency. 

The village is notable for its religious and cultural heritage, featuring four major temples—Kootumuchi Temple, Kurooramma Srikrishna Temple, Erakad Subramnaswami Temple, and Sree Rama Temple—each hosting annual festivals with traditional processions, music, and fireworks. Vellattanjur is also home to the Fathima Matha Church, the first church in India dedicated to Our Lady of Fatima, which celebrates grand feasts in October and January. The church also operates a dispensary, serving the local community.

Jimmy Turnbull (23 May 1882 – ?) was a Scottish footballer who played as a forward. He began his career in Scotland before moving to England, where he won the Football League title, FA Charity Shield, and FA Cup with Manchester United (1907–1910). He later played for Bradford Park Avenue and Chelsea, scoring 45 goals in 78 appearances for United. His brothers Tom and Alex were also footballers.
Stephen Tallents (born in London) was a British civil servant, military officer, and public relations pioneer, known for his leadership at the Empire Marketing Board and contributions to the BBC.

The 22nd Seanad was the upper house of the Oireachtas (Irish legislature) from 2002 to 2007. It consisted of 60 members: 43 elected from vocational panels, 6 from universities, and 11 nominated by the Taoiseach. Several senators were elected to the Dáil in 2007, leaving vacancies until the Seanad's dissolution.

The 1937–38 season in Mandatory Palestine football included the second part of the 1937 Palestine League, a new second division, and the 1938 Palestine Cup final won by Hapoel Tel Aviv. The national team participated in 1938 World Cup qualification, losing to Greece in the first round.

John Hull Campbell (October 10, 1800 – January 19, 1868) was an American lawyer and politician who served as a U.S. Representative from Pennsylvania's 3rd district from 1845 to 1847. A member of the American Party, he also served in the Pennsylvania House of Representatives in 1831. After declining renomination to Congress, he resumed his law practice in Philadelphia, where he lived until his death.

Mieczyslaw "Mickey Doyle" Kuzik is a fictional character from the HBO series "Boardwalk Empire," portrayed by Paul Sparks. Introduced as a Polish-American gangster, Mickey seeks to establish himself in the illegal alcohol trade during Prohibition. Throughout the series, he navigates alliances and rivalries with notable figures such as Nucky Thompson and Jimmy Darmody, ultimately becoming a key player in Atlantic City's criminal underworld. His character is loosely based on real-life mobster Mickey Duffy.

The Philadelphia Jewish Film Festival, founded in 1981, is the second-oldest Jewish film festival in the United States. Hosted by the Gershman Y in Philadelphia, it showcases Jewish-themed films annually, featuring international works and audience awards.

The history of Koreans in Houston dates back to the 1970s, when the Korean population was around 2,090. By 2000, this number had grown to 8,764, comprising 4.5% of the county's Asian population. Significant milestones include the establishment of Korean organizations like the Korean American Society of Houston (KASH) and the Korean Community Center of Houston (KCCH). The Korean community faces challenges such as political representation and religious discord. Today, Houston hosts various Korean cultural events and institutions, including the Korean Festival (K-Fest) and multiple Korean churches.

Vena, originally known as D'Element, was a bachata group formed in the Bronx, New York, in 2011 by Lenny and Max Santos, along with Steven Tejada. The group gained popularity with hits like "Señora" and "Ya No." After experiencing lineup changes, including Tejada's departure in 2015, Vena rebranded as DNA Latin Group in 2018, releasing new singles such as "Lujuria" and "Soy Tu Delirio." The group was active until 2019.

O RLY? is a phrase popularized by a meme and has been referenced in various contexts, including video games like World of Warcraft, where characters O’Reely and Yarly make reference to "O RLY?" and "YA RLY!". It gained notoriety after the discovery of the W32/Hoots-A computer worm in 2006, which displayed the phrase "O RLY?" when infecting Windows-based computers. The meme has also been parodied on book covers by O’Reilly Media, first popularized by a meme generator created by Ben Halpern.

The Stockade Building System consists of fibrous blocks with large interstices, coated with plaster and designed for sawing to accommodate floor joists. These blocks, when filled with concrete, provide a lightweight yet sturdy construction method. Developed by Fuller and Hewlett, the system was patented on June 28, 1927, as an extension of earlier patents.
The MAGI '900 Museum is a contemporary art museum in Italy, housed in a converted 1930s silo.

The 152 mm howitzer-gun M1937 (ML-20) is a Soviet artillery piece designed by F. F. Petrov and produced from 1937 to 1947, with a total of 6,884 units manufactured. Classified as a howitzer-gun, it combines features of both howitzers and guns, allowing for versatile use in various combat roles. The ML-20 saw extensive service during the Winter War and World War II, known for its reliability and effectiveness against enemy fortifications and armored vehicles. It was eventually replaced by the D-20 gun but remained in service with various countries and saw action in multiple conflicts, including the Arab-Israeli wars.

The Jamaica Premier League (JPL) is the top-tier professional football league in Jamaica, established in 1973. Featuring 12 clubs, it operates under a promotion and relegation system with regional leagues. Portmore United holds the record for the most championships with seven titles. The league has produced four Caribbean champions and provides qualification pathways to the CFU Club Championship and CONCACAF competitions.

Richard Garin (born December 11, 1967) is a Filipino politician and civil engineer serving as the mayor of Miagao since 2022. Previously, he was a member of the Philippine House of Representatives (2013–2019), Vice Governor of Iloilo (2010–2013), and mayor of Guimbal (1998–2007). He is married to former Health Secretary Janette Garin.

Trapping the queen is a chess tactic where a player forces the opponent's queen into a position where it cannot escape capture. Notable examples include games like Alekhine-Rubinstein (1930) and Nezhmetdinov-Konstantinov (1936), where decisive moves led to the queen being trapped and lost. The tactic often involves sacrifices to lure the queen into a vulnerable position.

Cephaloridine is a cephalosporin antibiotic introduced in 1962, known for its effectiveness against various bacterial infections, particularly those caused by penicillin-resistant strains. While it was initially used to treat urinary tract and respiratory infections, its popularity declined due to poor oral absorption and potential kidney toxicity. Today, it is primarily utilized in veterinary medicine.

John Dinham (1359–1428) was a prominent English nobleman and landowner from the Dinham family, which originated in Brittany and held significant estates in Devon, Somerset, and Cornwall. After inheriting his father's lands and half of his mother's estate, Dinham faced legal disputes with Hartland Abbey, where his family had founded the abbey. Known for his violent nature, he killed one of the men responsible for his father's murder and was later pardoned. Dinham married three times, fathering a son and a daughter. His effigy and those of his wives survive in St Mary's Church, Kingskerswell.

Gareth James Delany (born 28 April 1997) is an Irish cricketer. He made his Twenty20 International debut for Ireland in July 2019 and his One Day International debut in January 2020. Domestically, he has played for Leinster Lightning, Leicestershire, and Munster Reds.

Michael Van Wijk (born 30 September 1952) is an English bodybuilder, actor, and former television personality, best known for his role as "Wolf" on the British TV series *Gladiators*. He ran gyms in London, later moved to New Zealand, and competed in Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu.
**Eddy L. Harris** (born January 26, 1956) is an American writer and filmmaker known for his autobiographical works.

An institute is a type of organization, often focused on research, education, or professional standards. In the United Kingdom and the Isle of Man, the term is legally protected and may only be used by organizations conducting high-level research or representing professional bodies of the highest standing. Businesses must obtain approval from the Secretary of State before using "institute" in their name under the Business Names Act. Unauthorized use constitutes a criminal offence.
Cardinal Newman High School is a Catholic school in Columbia, South Carolina, for grades 7–12. Established in 1858, it moved to a new campus near Sesquicentennial State Park in 2016.

CNN Pipeline was an internet streaming television service launched by CNN on December 5, 2005, offering live video feeds, archived content, and news programming through a subscription model. It featured four simultaneous video streams, known as "pipes," showcasing live news events, interviews, and special programming. Anchored by Veronica De La Cruz, Nicole Lapin, Melissa Long, and Richard Lui, the service operated from CNN's virtual studio. On June 27, 2007, CNN discontinued Pipeline, transitioning its content to a free, ad-supported format on CNN.com. In 2014, CNNgo was introduced as its successor, incorporating similar features.

Middle Scots was the Anglic language spoken and written in the Scottish Lowlands and parts of the Northern Isles from the late 14th century to the mid-18th century, evolving into Modern Scots. It developed from Early Scots, which itself originated from Northumbrian Old English. By the early 16th century, the term "Scottis" referred to this language, distinguishing it from Gaelic, which was then called "Erse" (Irish). Middle Scots saw significant southern English influence following the Union of the Crowns in 1603 and the Union of Parliaments in 1707, leading to the adoption of Modern English as the literary standard, while Scots remained a vernacular language.

*Phebalium nottii*, commonly known as the **pink phebalium**, is a shrub in the family Rutaceae and is endemic to inland Queensland and New South Wales. It typically grows to 1–3 m (3 ft 3 in – 9 ft 10 in) high and has pink to deep mauve flowers that occur in spring.

The Newmarket Viaduct, constructed between 1962 and 1966, is New Zealand's first pair of balanced cantilever bridges. Opened on September 3, 1966, at a cost of NZ$2.26 million, it was designed to survive a 1-in-500-year earthquake. Due to increasing traffic demands and structural limitations, plans were made for a new viaduct capable of withstanding a 2,500-year return period earthquake. The new structure, costing approximately NZ$195 million, was completed in March 2013.

Ludvík Podéšť (1923–1995) studied music composition and musicology, directed youth choirs, and composed opera, film music, and orchestral works, blending classical and folk influences while engaging with political themes.

Neodiprion abietis, the balsam fir sawfly, is a species of sawfly whose larvae feed on conifers, particularly balsam fir. It undergoes five to six larval instars, exhibits arrhenotokous parthenogenesis, and causes periodic outbreaks in eastern Canada, leading to tree defoliation and growth reduction.

Sir Frederick Mann (2 May 1869 – 29 May 1958) was a prominent Australian jurist and public figure. Born in Mount Gambier, South Australia, Mann served as the Chief Justice of Victoria from October 1935 to January 1944 and Lieutenant Governor of Victoria from 22 July 1919 to 12 May 1936. A distinguished legal scholar, he was knighted in 1933 and is remembered for his contributions to judicial interpretation, particularly in the case Trustee's Executors & Agency Co Ltd v Reilly, where he defined the phrase "in respect of." Mann's tenure was marked by his critical stance on police investigative methods and his dedication to public service.

Barley yellow mosaic virus (BaYMV) is a systemic plant pathogenic virus primarily affecting barley (Hordeum vulgare) and transmitted by the protist Polymyxa graminis. The virus causes distinctive symptoms, including yellowing and mosaic patterns on leaves, leaf curl, stunting, and necrosis. BaYMV is prevalent in Eastern Asia and Western Europe, thriving in cold winters and mild, wet autumns. Once a plant is infected, it remains so for its lifetime, with no known cure, making prevention crucial. Resistance through specific barley cultivars has shown some success, but emerging virulent strains pose ongoing challenges. The virus significantly impacts barley yields, leading to substantial economic losses.
Al-Majd, a village council in Hebron, Palestine, has a population of 1,925 (2007) and was under Jordanian rule from 1949 to 1967 before coming under Israeli occupation.
The Rechtboomssloot is a historic canal in Amsterdam, dating to the 16th century, named after shipbuilder Cornelis Boom.

Maya Aleksandrovna Ulanovskaya (1932–2020) was a Soviet and Israeli literary critic, translator, and teacher. Born in New York City to Soviet spies, she was later arrested in the USSR for anti-Stalinist activities and sentenced to the Gulag. After release and emigration to Israel, she translated works from English, Hebrew, and Yiddish into Russian.
Eleanor Perenyi (January 4, 1918 – May 3, 2009) was an American author best known for *Green Thoughts: A Writer in the Garden* and her 1946 memoir *More Was Lost*, chronicling her life in Hungary.
Todd Scott is a New Zealand media executive, former television host, and butcher, owning the *National Business Review*.

Terry Mutchler began her career as a journalist, covering politics for the Associated Press in various states and winning multiple awards. She later attended John Marshall School of Law and worked in government roles under the Clinton administration. In 2008, she became the first director of Pennsylvania's Office of Open Records, significantly improving public access and transparency. Mutchler has also been involved in legal cases, including representing senators in domestic abuse and corruption allegations. Her work has been recognized with several awards for public service and government transparency. In 2014, she published a memoir detailing her long-term relationship with Illinois Senator Penny Severns.

Muslim Community Radio, owned by the Islamic Forum Europe and based at the London Muslim Centre near the East London Mosque, began broadcasting in 1998. Operating on AM 1143, it offers variety programming in English and Bengali, including women's programmes, children's shows, quizzes, and religious sessions, primarily during Ramadan.

Melanie Susan Oxley is an Australian musician, singer-songwriter, and primary school teacher, known for her work in rock, dance pop, and soul pop genres. She began her music career in the 1980s, performing with bands such as Paris Green and The Sparklers, before forming a long-standing duo with Chris Abrahams in 1989. Together, they released several critically acclaimed albums, including *Welcome to Violet* (1992), which was nominated for Best Independent Release at the ARIA Music Awards. Outside of music, Oxley is a primary school teacher and mother of three, residing in Sydney, Australia.

The Silent Sejm, held in February 1717, was a controversial assembly convened to prevent the use of the liberum veto and ensure the settlement of disputes between Augustus II of Poland and the Polish nobility. Threatened by a strong Russian presence, the Sejm was conducted in silence, allowing only the speaker and a few selected deputies to voice their opinions. The settlement, heavily influenced by Peter the Great, reduced the size of the Commonwealth's army and officially recognized Russia as a guarantor of the agreement. While some beneficial reforms were enacted, the Sejm is seen as a precursor to Russian intervention in Polish affairs and a step towards the eventual partitions of Poland, marking the end of Poland's independence.

The November 1992 Venezuelan coup attempt was the second coup attempt that year against President Carlos Andrés Pérez. Led by air force and navy officers, the rebels took over a state-run television station and seized air bases. The coup ultimately failed, resulting in 172 deaths, and many participants fled to Peru.

Han Lao Da, born Ann Jong Juan (Hán Yǒng Yuán) on October 22, 1947, in Singapore, is a Singaporean xiangsheng playwright and promoter. He befriended prominent xiangsheng artists in the 1980s and composed numerous scripts, winning local awards and publishing xiangsheng collections. Han also contributes to the Singaporean drama scene as a playwright.

USS Romulus (LST-962) was a tank landing ship built in 1944 and commissioned in 1944. During World War II, it served in the Pacific, providing tender and repair services. After the war, it was converted to a repair ship (ARL-22) and saw service in the Korean War. Decommissioned in 1956, it was transferred to the Philippine Navy in 1961, serving as Aklan (AR-67) and later Kamagong until 1989. Romulus earned one battle star for its service in the Korean War.

Arkady Adamovich Brish (May 14, 1917 – March 19, 2016) was a Soviet and Russian physicist and resistance fighter. Born in Minsk, he graduated from the Physics and Mathematics Faculty of Belarusian State University and worked as an electrician and in the sciences. During World War II, he fought against Nazi invaders. He received numerous awards for his service and died in Moscow at age 98. A memorial plaque honoring him was installed in 2017.

*Uncle Jam Wants You* is a studio album by Funkadelic, released on September 21, 1979, by Warner Bros. Produced by George Clinton, the album is a militant sequel to *One Nation Under a Groove*, with themes of converting listeners to "Funkadelia." The album cover depicts Clinton in a Huey Newton-Black Panthers pose, reflecting the album's martial themes.

*Uncle Jam Wants You* featured the band's last major hit, "(Not Just) Knee Deep," which reached number one on the Billboard Soul singles chart. The album significantly influenced the West Coast hip-hop scene, notably the DJ organization Uncle Jamm's Army. Samples of "(Not Just) Knee Deep" have been used in songs by De La Soul and Dr. Dre.

*Marguerite* is a musical with music by Michel Legrand, lyrics by Alain Boublil and Herbert Kretzmer, and a book by Alain Boublil, Claude-Michel Schönberg, and Jonathan Kent. Based on *La Dame aux Camélias*, it premiered in London's West End in 2008. A revised version with a rewritten book and new musical numbers was produced in the Czech Republic in 2010. Subsequent productions and a London revival featured elements from both versions. The story is set in Paris during World War II.

The DR Class 52.80 was a class of 200 "Rekolok" (reconstructed locomotives) rebuilt from DRB Class 52 locomotives by the Deutsche Reichsbahn (DR) in East Germany between 1960 and 1967. The rebuild included a new combustion-chambered boiler, welded cylinders, and a new driver's cab. A number of these locomotives survive today, including one extensively rebuilt example owned by DLM AG in Switzerland.

Jefferson Andrés Lerma Solís (born 25 October 1994) is a Colombian professional footballer who plays as a defensive midfielder for Premier League club AFC Bournemouth and the Colombia national team. Lerma began his career with Atlético Huila in Colombia, where he made his professional debut in 2013 and became a key player. In 2015, he joined Spanish club Levante, initially on loan, and helped them achieve promotion to La Liga during the 2016–17 season. 

In 2018, Lerma signed with Bournemouth for a club-record fee, becoming a fan favorite for his tough-tackling style. He played a pivotal role in Bournemouth's promotion back to the Premier League in the 2021–22 season. Internationally, Lerma has represented Colombia since 2017, featuring in the 2018 FIFA World Cup and the 2019 Copa América. Known for his versatility and physicality, he has also scored key goals for both club and country.

The Acheloos Painter was an anonymous Greek vase painter active around 525 to 500 BCE, known for his contributions to the black-figure style and association with the Leagros Group. He is best recognized for the "name vase," an amphora depicting a comic scene of Herakles battling the river god Acheloos, portrayed as a horned centaur. This work, along with other scenes, reflects a blend of heroic and humorous elements in Greek mythology.

May Song Vang (February 5, 1951 – August 5, 2013) was a Laotian-born Hmong American community leader, educator, and healthcare worker. She became a pioneering figure in Laos as a trained midwife and physician assistant before marrying General Vang Pao in 1973. After fleeing to the U.S. in 1975, she advocated for Hmong refugees, promoted education, and helped establish Vang Pao Elementary School in Fresno, California. She founded the General Vang Pao Foundation and remained a key leader in the Hmong American community until her death.

The European Data Portal is a centralized platform providing access to open public sector data from EU member states and EFTA countries. It aggregates metadata from national portals to promote data reuse and was consolidated into data.europa.eu in 2021.

The Murakumo-class destroyers were a class of six warships built for the Imperial Japanese Navy, commissioned from December 1898 to June 1925. Designed by John I. Thornycroft & Company, these vessels were notable for their speed and torpedo capabilities, participating in key conflicts such as the Russo-Japanese War and World War I.
Sthenelaidas was a Spartan ephor in 432/431 BC who played a key role in pushing Sparta toward war with Athens, as recorded in Thucydides' *History of the Peloponnesian War*.

Mothers against decapentaplegic homolog 7, or SMAD7, is a protein that inhibits TGF-β signaling by preventing the formation of Smad2/Smad4 complexes. It interacts with activated TGF-β type I receptor, blocking the association, phosphorylation, and activation of Smad2. SMAD7 also plays a role in negative feedback for Activin and bone morphogenetic protein pathways. It is induced by stimuli such as epidermal growth factor, interferon-γ, and tumor necrosis factor-α, providing cross-talk between TGF-β signaling and other cellular signaling pathways. A mutation in the SMAD7 gene is linked to susceptibility to colorectal cancer.

Wesley Bolin Memorial Plaza is a public space in Phoenix, Arizona, established in 1978 and named for Governor Wesley Bolin. It houses 30 memorials, including one to the USS Arizona, and has served as a site for protests and demonstrations. Some of the monuments have been the subject of controversy.

The University of St. Thomas School of Law, located in Minneapolis, Minnesota, is a private law school established in 1999, with its first graduating class in 2004. Accredited by the American Bar Association in 2006 and a member of the Association of American Law Schools since 2012, the school emphasizes practical training, social justice, and mentorship. Its Mentor Externship Program, which pairs students with legal professionals, has received national recognition. The school offers various degree programs, including J.D., combined degrees, and LL.M. programs. It is also home to notable centers and publications focused on ethics, law, and public policy.

The Bryant Chucking Grinder Company was founded in 1909 by William LeRoy (Roy) Bryant, a former chief engineer at Jones & Lamson Machine Company (J&L). Bryant invented a multispindle grinder and established the company in Springfield, Vermont, a hub for machine tool manufacturers. By the 1910s, the company had become a notable brand in both internal and external grinding, particularly for the automotive and aircraft industries. World War I further boosted demand, and by 1940, the company had expanded significantly. During World War II, Bryant produced critical machinery and expanded its workforce. Post-war, Bryant continued to grow, diversifying into computer drum memory and acquiring Van Norman Machine Tool Company in 1972. In 1986, the company was acquired by Textron, which later sold it to the Goldman Group. Financial challenges led to a brief closure in 2002, but Vermont Machine Tool subsequently acquired the company and continued operations. In 2015, Fives acquired Bryant's technology and intellectual property, continuing its legacy in the CNC grinding market.
